## Student's T-Distribution

*Student's T-distribution* (or simply T-distribution) is a probability distribution that is symmetric around zero and bell-shaped, like the standard normal distribution, but with heavier tails. The T-distribution $t_\nu$ (where $\nu$ are the degrees of freedom) approaches the standard normal distribution as $\nu\to\infty$. The name "Student" is a pseudonym for the English statistician William Sealy Gosset.

### Probability Density Function (PDF)

$f(t)=\frac{\Gamma(\frac{\nu+1}{2})}{\sqrt{\pi\nu}\Gamma(\frac{\nu}{2})}(1+\frac{t^2}{\nu})^{-(\nu+1)/2}$

Where:
* $\Gamma$ is the gamma function
* $\nu$ is the number of degrees of freedom

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import t

x = np.linspace(-4, 4, 100)
z = t.pdf(x, df=np.inf)
t_2 = t.pdf(x, df=2)
t_1 = t.pdf(x, df=1)
plt.plot(x,z,label="nu = infinity")
plt.plot(x,t_2, label="nu = 2")
plt.plot(x,t_1, label="nu = 1")
plt.legend()
```

```
<matplotlib.legend.Legend at 0x7298a64aef90>
```

```
<Figure size 640x480 with 1 Axes>
```

<img alt="No description has been provided for this image" class="" src="data:image/png;base64,"/>

## T-score

Formula: $\large t_{n-1}=\frac{\bar{X}-\mu}{s/\sqrt{n}}$

The T-score quantifies how many estimated standard errors the sample mean ($\bar{X}$) is from the population mean ($\mu$). It plays a role in hypothesis testing, confidence intervals or linear regression analysis. The T-distribution provides a way to draw inference about population means based on small samples.

**Assumption**: The population is normally distributed.

**Use cases**: Use the T-distribution when samples are small and population variance is unknown.

## T-table

Similar to the Z-table for the standard normal distribution, there's also a T-table for student's T-distribution. In contrast to the Z-table where the fields are the outcomes from the cumulative distribution function, the T-table provides T-scores from different T-distributions, for different levels of significance. The table's indices indicate the degrees of freedom, the columns indicate common values for the significance level $\alpha$, and the fields are the corresponding T-scores.

```python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 10))
ax.imshow(plt.imread("t-table.png"))
ax.set_axis_off()
```

```
<Figure size 1000x1000 with 1 Axes>
```

<img alt="No description has been provided for this image" class="" src="data:image/png;base64,"/>

Source: [https://www.tdistributiontable.com](https://www.tdistributiontable.com)

The corresponding T-score of a cumulative probability is given by the quantile function of the T-distribution.

$t=qt(p,df)$

* $p$ is the probability of observing a value lower or equal to $t$. $P(T \leq t)$
* $df$ are the degrees of freedom.

$F(t\|\nu)=\int_{-\infty}^{t}f(x)dx=p$

* $F(t\|\nu)$ is the cumulative distribution function of a T-distribution with $\nu$ degrees of freedom.
* $f(x)$ is the probability density function.

The quantile function is the inverse of the cumulative distribution function. In simple terms, the CDF takes a T-score as input and returns a probability. The quantile function takes a probability as input and returns a T-score.

$t=F^{-1}(p\|\nu)$

#### In Python you can simply use the Scipy library to get the T-score:

```python
from scipy.stats import t

alpha = 0.05
n = 10
t.ppf(1-alpha, n-1)
```

```
np.float64(1.8331129326536335)
```

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

confidence = np.array([60, 65, 70, 75, 80, 85, 90, 95, 99])

# create the t-table
t_table = pd.DataFrame(data=[], index=np.arange(1, 31),
                       columns=((100 - confidence) / 100) / 2)
for idx in t_table.index:
    for col in t_table.columns:
        critical_t = t.ppf(1-col, idx)
        t_table.loc[idx, col] = np.round(critical_t, 6)
t_table.loc[len(t_table) + 1] = [f"{i}%" for i in confidence]

# highlight the t-score from the above example
def format_func(x):
    fmap = []
    for i,_ in x.items():
        if x.name == alpha and i == n-1:
            fmap.append("background-color: lightgreen; color: black")
        else:
            fmap.append(None)
    return fmap

t_table.style.apply(format_func)
```

}

      <th id="T_5547e_level0_row15" class="row_heading level0 row15" >16</th>
      <td id="T_5547e_row15_col0" class="data row15 col0" >0.864667</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row15_col1" class="data row15 col1" >0.962748</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row15_col2" class="data row15 col2" >1.071137</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row15_col3" class="data row15 col3" >1.193685</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row15_col4" class="data row15 col4" >1.336757</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row15_col5" class="data row15 col5" >1.512130</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row15_col6" class="data row15 col6" >1.745884</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row15_col7" class="data row15 col7" >2.119905</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row15_col8" class="data row15 col8" >2.920782</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <th id="T_5547e_level0_row16" class="row_heading level0 row16" >17</th>
      <td id="T_5547e_row16_col0" class="data row16 col0" >0.863279</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row16_col1" class="data row16 col1" >0.961046</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row16_col2" class="data row16 col2" >1.069033</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row16_col3" class="data row16 col3" >1.191047</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row16_col4" class="data row16 col4" >1.333379</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row16_col5" class="data row16 col5" >1.507660</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row16_col6" class="data row16 col6" >1.739607</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row16_col7" class="data row16 col7" >2.109816</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row16_col8" class="data row16 col8" >2.898231</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <th id="T_5547e_level0_row17" class="row_heading level0 row17" >18</th>
      <td id="T_5547e_row17_col0" class="data row17 col0" >0.862049</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row17_col1" class="data row17 col1" >0.959539</td>

      <td id="T_5547e_row30_col6" class="data row30 col6" >90%</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row30_col7" class="data row30 col7" >95%</td>
      <td id="T_5547e_row30_col8" class="data row30 col8" >99%</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>


### Conclusion

Student's T-distribution is essential in inferential statistics, used to draw inference about population means. It is particularly useful when the sample size is small (<30) and population variance is unknown. T-scores are used as critical values in confidence intervals or as test statistics in a T-test.
